title: Scientists Discover Water on Mars Surface
description: NASA researchers confirm liquid water exists beneath the Martian south pole.
fullContent: Scientists at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory have confirmed the presence of liquid water beneath the surface of Mars. Using radar data from the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter, researchers identified a 20-kilometer-wide lake of liquid water approximately 1.5 kilometers beneath the Martian south pole. The discovery, published in the journal Science, represents a significant milestone in the search for extraterrestrial life. Dr. Elena Rodriguez, lead author of the study, stated that while the water is likely too cold and salty to support life as we know it, the finding opens new possibilities for understanding planetary habitability.

title: European Central Bank Raises Interest Rates by 0.25%
description: The ECB announced a quarter-point rate increase amid persistent inflation concerns.
fullContent: The European Central Bank raised its key interest rates by 25 basis points on Thursday, bringing the main deposit facility rate to 4.0%. ECB President Christine Lagarde stated that the decision reflects the governing council's commitment to returning inflation to the 2% medium-term target. The move was widely anticipated by markets following a series of stronger-than-expected inflation readings across the eurozone. Analysts at Deutsche Bank noted that the decision was unanimous among council members. The euro strengthened slightly against the dollar following the announcement, trading at 1.0842.

title: New Study Links Mediterranean Diet to Reduced Alzheimer's Risk
description: Researchers at Harvard Medical School found a 23% reduction in dementia risk among adherents.
fullContent: A comprehensive study conducted by researchers at Harvard Medical School and published in the New England Journal of Medicine has found that adherence to a Mediterranean-style diet is associated with a 23% reduction in the risk of developing Alzheimer's disease. The study followed 8,000 participants aged 65 and older over a period of 12 years. Participants who closely followed the Mediterranean diet — rich in olive oil, fish, vegetables, legumes, and whole grains — showed significantly better cognitive outcomes compared to those following a standard Western diet. Dr. James Chen, the study's principal investigator, noted that the findings add to a growing body of evidence supporting dietary interventions as a low-cost, accessible strategy for dementia prevention.
